key_insight: A microscopic constitutive model links nonaffine elasticity and structural relaxation to explain stress overshoot, yielding, steady flow, and polymer strain hardening in amorphous solids.
engineering_relevance: The framework offers a physically grounded way to predict full stress--strain curves for metallic and polymer glasses across strain rates using a small number of measurable parameters.
method: Nonaffine elastic response is coupled to irreversible many-body relaxation.
Stress overshoot arises from competition between mechanical connectivity loss and viscous dissipation.
Polymer chain extensibility is included to capture strain hardening at large deformation.
evidence_signal: The model quantitatively reproduces experimental stress--strain data for metallic glasses, polycarbonate, PMMA, and epoxy resins over a broad range of strain rates.
watch_item: Accuracy depends on independently measured relaxation exponents and material parameters, so transferability may be limited if these inputs are not available or if the loading path differs strongly from the calibration cases.
paper_title: Microscopic constitutive theory of stress overshoot, yielding, and strain hardening in amorphous materials
